name.en: Accademia di Belle Arti di Brera
name.it: Accademia di Belle Arti di Brera

note.en: Italy's most storied art school, founded 1776.
note.it: La più storica accademia d'arte d'Italia, fondata nel 1776.

intro.en: Generations of students have learned to see beneath my arcades. Every morning at eight, the scent of turpentine blends with coffee from the dairy across the way. Walk slowly — I've learned to recognise the footsteps of lovers here.
intro.it: Sotto i miei portici, generazioni di studenti hanno imparato a guardare. Ogni mattina alle otto, l'odore di trementina si mescola al caffè della latteria di fronte. Cammina lentamente — qui ho imparato a riconoscere i passi degli innamorati.

voice.en: Walk beneath my arcades. I'll tell you who has passed through.
voice.it: Cammina sotto i miei portici. Ti dirò chi è passato di qui.

landmarks.short_description: Italy's most storied art school, founded 1776 by Maria Theresa of Austria.
landmarks.long_description: The Accademia shares Palazzo Brera with the Pinacoteca and the Orto Botanico. Founded in 1776, it has trained generations of Italian painters, sculptors and set designers — Hayez, Boccioni, Funi, Castiglioni among them. Students still walk in and out at all hours, sketchbooks under their arms. The arcaded courtyard is open to the public; the studios upstairs are not, but on warm days you can smell the turpentine through the windows.
landmarks.fun_fact: Marina Abramović studied here briefly in the 1970s.
canonical_facts: Founded in 1776 by Empress Maria Theresa of Austria.
Shares Palazzo Brera with the Pinacoteca and the Orto Botanico.
Notable alumni include Francesco Hayez, Umberto Boccioni and Achille Castiglioni.

note.en: Raphael, Mantegna and Caravaggio under one ceiling.
note.it: Raffaello, Mantegna e Caravaggio sotto lo stesso soffitto.

intro.en: I keep Raphael's Marriage of the Virgin, Mantegna's Dead Christ, Caravaggio's Supper at Emmaus. But the real show is the courtyard: in spring, Napoleon's magnolia blooms above your shoulders.
intro.it: Custodisco lo Sposalizio della Vergine di Raffaello, il Cristo morto di Mantegna, la Cena in Emmaus di Caravaggio. Ma il vero spettacolo è il cortile: in primavera la magnolia di Napoleone fiorisce sopra le tue spalle.

voice.en: I have kept their silences. Come and feel them.
voice.it: Ho conservato i loro silenzi. Vieni a sentirli.

landmarks.short_description: Raphael, Mantegna and Caravaggio under one ceiling — Milan's painting museum.
landmarks.long_description: Founded by Napoleon in 1809 to centralise the artworks confiscated from suppressed religious orders, the Pinacoteca holds masterpieces including Raphael's Marriage of the Virgin, Mantegna's Lamentation of Christ, and Caravaggio's Supper at Emmaus. The neoclassical courtyard at the entrance features Antonio Canova's monumental bronze of Napoleon as Mars the Peacemaker, twice life-size. In spring the magnolia in the courtyard blooms above visitors' shoulders.
landmarks.fun_fact: The Mantegna's Dead Christ uses radical foreshortening from a viewpoint near the feet.
canonical_facts: Founded by Napoleon in 1809 to centralise art confiscated from suppressed religious orders.
Holds Raphael's Marriage of the Virgin, Mantegna's Lamentation, and Caravaggio's Supper at Emmaus.
Antonio Canova's bronze Napoleon as Mars the Peacemaker stands twice life-size in the courtyard.

note.en: Two facades, two architects, one quiet quarrel.
note.it: Due facciate, due architetti, un litigio in pietra.

intro.en: Look at me from Via Brera, then turn the corner. I look like a different building, don't I? Two architects — Ruggeri in the 1700s, Piermarini a century later — never agreed. I've housed the Military Command since 1860, but I still remember the ball for Maria Theresa of Austria.
intro.it: Guardami da via Brera, poi gira l'angolo. Sembro un altro edificio, vero? È perché due architetti — Ruggeri nel '700, Piermarini un secolo dopo — non si sono mai messi d'accordo. Sono sede del Comando Militare dal 1860, ma ricordo ancora la festa per Maria Teresa d'Austria.

voice.en: I wear two faces. Come and find out why.
voice.it: Ho due volti. Vieni a scoprire perché.

landmarks.short_description: An 18th-century palace with two different facades by two architects.
landmarks.long_description: Built in the early 1700s by Giovanni Ruggeri for the Cusani family. A century later, when Maria Theresa of Austria visited, Giuseppe Piermarini was hired to remodel the rear elevation in a more neoclassical style — but he never harmonised it with Ruggeri's Baroque front. The result: two facades, two eras, one quiet quarrel in stone. Since 1860 the palace has housed the Italian Army's Northern Command, but the great staircase and ballrooms still survive.
landmarks.fun_fact: The two facades are sometimes used as an architecture-school example of how NOT to remodel.

note.en: Versace's HQ since 1980 — frescoed ceilings still intact.
note.it: Sede Versace dal 1980 — soffitti affrescati ancora intatti.

intro.en: For nearly fifty years the Versace maison has stitched its dreams behind my doors. Above the rooms where Donatella picks fabrics, my eighteenth-century ceilings still show their frescoed gods. Few know that behind these closed shutters once lived a woman astronomer.
intro.it: Da quasi cinquant'anni la maison Versace cuce i suoi sogni dietro i miei portoni. Sopra le sale dove Donatella sceglie i tessuti, i miei soffitti del Settecento mostrano ancora le loro divinità affrescate. Pochi sanno che dietro le persiane chiuse, una volta, abitava un'astronoma.

voice.en: Beneath today's fashions, I keep older stories.
voice.it: Sotto le mode di oggi, conservo storie più antiche.

landmarks.short_description: Versace's headquarters since 1980 — frescoed 18th-century ceilings still intact.
landmarks.long_description: Built in the 16th century, redecorated in the 1700s with frescoed ceilings depicting mythological scenes. Gianni Versace bought the palace in 1980 and made it the headquarters of the Versace fashion house. Donatella Versace still walks these halls. Few know that one wing was once inhabited by Maria Gaetana Agnesi, the 18th-century mathematician and astronomer, before she renounced worldly life and became a nun.
landmarks.fun_fact: The garden behind the palace is one of the largest private green spaces in central Milan.

note.en: The opera house that crowns the southern edge of Brera.
note.it: Il teatro d'opera che corona il bordo sud di Brera.

intro.en: On 7 December, Milan dresses up for me. Beneath my stage Maria Callas and Luciano Pavarotti have sung — and before them Maria Malibran, who died at 28. But pass by at six in the morning and you'll hear the cleaning women sing their own version of La Traviata.
intro.it: Il 7 dicembre, Milano si veste per me. Sotto il mio palco hanno cantato Maria Callas e Luciano Pavarotti, e prima di loro Maria Malibran morta a 28 anni. Ma se passi alle sei del mattino, sentirai le donne delle pulizie cantare la loro versione della Traviata.

voice.en: My notes begin long before the curtain.
voice.it: Le mie note iniziano molto prima del sipario.

landmarks.short_description: The opera house that crowns the southern edge of Brera.
landmarks.long_description: Inaugurated 3 August 1778 with Antonio Salieri's Europa Riconosciuta, designed by Giuseppe Piermarini on the site of the demolished church of Santa Maria alla Scala. La Scala has hosted the world premieres of operas by Verdi (Otello, Falstaff, Nabucco), Puccini (Madama Butterfly, Turandot), and Bellini (Norma). Maria Callas redefined operatic acting from this stage in the 1950s. The opening night each season — 7 December, the feast of Sant'Ambrogio — is when Milan dresses itself for the year.
landmarks.fun_fact: Toscanini's baton is preserved in the museum on the left side of the lobby.

pois.name: Orto Botanico di Brera
pois.short_description: A secret 18th-century botanical garden tucked behind Palazzo Brera.
pois.long_description: Founded in 1774 by Maria Theresa of Austria, this hidden 5,000 sqm garden hosts ancient ginkgo trees, medicinal plants and a quiet pond. Few tourists know it exists.
pois.fun_fact: Two of its ginkgo biloba trees are over 240 years old.

opening_line.en: Beyond this gate, a 240-year-old ginkgo is waiting for you to look up.

pois.name: Cortile della Pinacoteca
pois.short_description: The arcaded courtyard with Napoleon as a Roman god.
pois.long_description: Inside Palazzo Brera, this neoclassical courtyard hosts a striking bronze nude of Napoleon by Antonio Canova. Free to enter even without a museum ticket.
pois.fun_fact: Canova depicted Napoleon idealised, twice life-size, holding a Nike.

opening_line.en: Step inside — Napoleon, twice life-size, has been waiting for you in bronze.

pois.name: Fioraio Bianchi Caffè
pois.short_description: A Parisian-style florist that became a tiny bistrot.
pois.long_description: Half flower shop, half restaurant — Raimondo Bianchi's 1976 atelier still arranges blooms among marble tables and serves a beloved seasonal lunch.
pois.fun_fact: Many Milanese still order their wedding bouquets here.

opening_line.en: Smell that? It's roses, and lunch, served at marble tables since 1976.

pois.name: Cimitero di San Marco (vestiges)
pois.short_description: Traces of a medieval graveyard hidden beneath the church square.
pois.long_description: The piazza in front of San Marco church covers the former parish cemetery. Look for worn tombstones reused as paving stones near the side wall.
pois.fun_fact: Mozart performed inside San Marco at age 14, in 1770.

opening_line.en: Look down — the stones beneath your feet were once tombstones. Mozart played near here at fourteen.

pois.name: Casa degli Atellani — Vigna di Leonardo
pois.short_description: Leonardo da Vinci's personal vineyard, replanted on the original soil.
pois.long_description: Just a few minutes from Brera, this Renaissance house preserves the vineyard gifted to Leonardo by Ludovico il Moro in 1498. The malvasia vines were genetically restored in 2015.
pois.fun_fact: Leonardo mentioned the vineyard in his will of 1519.

opening_line.en: Leonardo's vines are still thirsty. The same soil he sketched in 1498 is right behind you.

pois.name: Libreria Bocca
pois.long_description: Hidden in Galleria Vittorio Emanuele but spiritually a Brera neighbour, Bocca has specialised in art, fine prints, and rare editions since the late 18th century.
pois.fun_fact: Futurist manifestos by Marinetti were printed and sold here.

opening_line.en: Pages from 1775 are turning, slowly. Marinetti's Futurist manifestos started right here.

pois.name: Vicolo dei Lavandai
pois.short_description: The last open-air laundry alley of Milan, on the Naviglio.
pois.long_description: Just south of Brera along the Naviglio Grande, this narrow lane preserves the 1700s wash-house where lavandai (male washermen) cleaned the city's laundry until the 1950s.
pois.fun_fact: It was protected as a monument in 1982 to save it from demolition.

opening_line.en: The last open-air laundry of Milan still echoes with washermen of the 1700s.

pois.name: Chiesa di San Carpoforo
pois.short_description: A deconsecrated 11th-century church now hosting Brera Academy exhibitions.
pois.long_description: One of the oldest churches in Milan, repurposed into an exhibition hall by the nearby Accademia di Belle Arti. Romanesque bones with contemporary art layered on top.

opening_line.en: An eleventh-century church is hosting today's young artists. Romanesque stones, modern hands.

pois.name: Bar Jamaica
pois.short_description: The 1920s artists' bar where Brera bohemia was born.
pois.long_description: Hemingway, Lucio Fontana, Quasimodo and Ungaretti all drank here. The walls are still hung with original photographs of Brera's mid-century painters and poets.
pois.fun_fact: It is the unofficial 'living room' of the Accademia di Belle Arti.

opening_line.en: Hemingway's table is empty, but the photographs on these walls are still watching.

pois.name: Palazzo Cusani
pois.short_description: An 18th-century palace with two contrasting facades.
pois.long_description: Designed by Giovanni Ruggeri (1719) on Via Brera and by Piermarini on the back — it is the only Milanese palace whose two main fronts come from rival baroque/neoclassical architects.
pois.hours: Open courtyard during army office hours
pois.fun_fact: Today it is the HQ of the Italian Army Northern Command.

opening_line.en: Two facades, two architects, one quiet quarrel in stone since 1719.

pois.name: Pasticceria Marchesi 1824
pois.short_description: Milan's oldest patisserie, where Verdi bought his cassoeula.
pois.long_description: Founded in 1824 with original walnut counters and pastel boiserie. The almond panettoncini are baked from the same recipe used for nearly two centuries.
pois.address: Via Santa Maria alla Porta 11A, 20123 Milano
pois.fun_fact: Now part of Prada — but the 1824 décor is preserved.

opening_line.en: Walnut counters from 1824. Ask for the panettoncini — Verdi did.

pois.name: Cortile della Magnolia
pois.short_description: A secret courtyard with a centuries-old magnolia tree.
pois.long_description: Step through an unassuming portone on Via dei Giardini and you'll find a peaceful private courtyard dominated by a vast flowering magnolia — an open secret among Brera locals.
pois.fun_fact: The magnolia blooms briefly in late March — a Brera ritual.

opening_line.en: A magnolia is blooming behind that unmarked door. Brera's open secret.

pois.name: Studio Museo Francesco Messina
pois.short_description: A sculptor's workshop turned museum inside a deconsecrated church.
pois.long_description: The 17th-century Chiesa di San Sisto houses 80 sculptures and 25 drawings by Francesco Messina, donated to the city in 1974. Almost always empty.
pois.fun_fact: The bronze horses outside RAI's Rome HQ are by Messina.

opening_line.en: A 17th-century church now holds 80 sculptures. You'll likely have it to yourself.

pois.name: Antica Barbieria Colla
pois.long_description: Mahogany counters, original Belle Époque mirrors and a hand-mixed shaving cream called 'Crema 1904'. Booking weeks ahead is the norm for the wet shave.
pois.fun_fact: Toscanini and Verdi were both Colla regulars.

opening_line.en: A wet shave from 1904 is being prepared. Toscanini knew this scent.

pois.name: Casa Museo Boschi Di Stefano
pois.short_description: A 1930s apartment-museum stuffed with 300 Italian modernist masterpieces.
pois.long_description: An almost-secret museum in a Piero Portaluppi flat, displaying works by Fontana, de Chirico, Sironi, Morandi and Boccioni — collected by a single Milanese couple.
pois.fun_fact: Entry is completely free — a bequest condition.

opening_line.en: Three hundred modernist masterpieces are quietly hung in someone's living room. Free, always.

pois.name: Latteria di San Marco
pois.short_description: A 12-table 1933 milk-bar, now serving Milanese home cooking.
pois.long_description: No reservations, no menu cards, just blackboard specials and risotto al salto. Run for decades by the Maggi family — a portrait of pre-aperitivo Brera.

opening_line.en: Twelve tables. No menu. The risotto al salto would like you to sit down.

pois.name: Chiostro dell'Umanitaria
pois.short_description: A Renaissance cloister hidden inside a working social institute.
pois.long_description: Two 15th-century cloisters with a peaceful well, often used for outdoor concerts. Walk straight in past the porter — almost no one realises it is open to the public.
pois.fun_fact: Originally part of a 15th-century Franciscan convent.

opening_line.en: Two Renaissance cloisters are open behind a porter's desk. Walk in.

pois.name: Via Bagnera
pois.short_description: Milan's narrowest street — and once its grisliest.
pois.long_description: A 2.5m-wide medieval alley between Via Nerino and Via Santa Marta. In 1861 it was the scene of Milan's first serial-killer case (Antonio Boggia).
pois.fun_fact: Boggia was the last person publicly executed in Milan.

opening_line.en: Milan's narrowest alley would like you to know it once held the city's last public execution.
